articles.title: Voter ID Required for GM Mayoral Election in Rochdale
articles.excerpt: Rochdale Borough Council reminds voters that photo ID is necessary for the upcoming GM Mayoral Election on 30 July. Those without ID can apply for a free Voter Authority Certificate before 22 July.
articles.content_html:
Rochdale Borough Council has announced that voters will need an accepted form of photo identification to vote at polling stations during the Greater Manchester Mayoral Election on 30 July.

Residents without an accepted form of ID are encouraged to apply for a free Voter Authority Certificate. Applications must be submitted before 5pm on 22 July to ensure eligibility to vote.

The council has provided information on how to obtain the Voter Authority Certificate, which can be found on their official website.

This requirement is part of new regulations aimed at ensuring the integrity of the voting process in the upcoming election.

A major redevelopment of Rochdale's iconic arts and heritage venue is nearing completion, with plans for it to open to the public shortly. The venue, which has been a significant part of the local cultural landscape, has undergone extensive renovations to modernise its facilities.

Details regarding the specific features of the redevelopment have not yet been disclosed, but the project aims to enhance the visitor experience and expand the range of activities offered.

Local officials have expressed optimism about the reopening, highlighting the venue's potential to attract visitors and support the local economy.

Further announcements regarding the exact opening date and planned events are expected in the coming weeks.

articles.content_html:
Rochdale Council has hired a contractor to complete the Touchstones project, which has faced delays. The decision aims to ensure the timely finishing of the cultural centre, significant for the local community.

The contractor will address outstanding work and bring the project to completion.

Further details regarding the timeline for completion and specific works to be undertaken by the contractor will be announced by the council in due course.

articles.title: New Contractor Appointed for Touchstones Rochdale Redevelopment
articles.excerpt: Schofield and Sons has been appointed to continue the redevelopment of Touchstones Rochdale, with work on the first floor galleries and roof repairs set to begin ahead of a planned reopening in summer 2027.
articles.content_html:
A new contractor has been appointed to oversee the next phase of the transformation of Touchstones Rochdale, with Schofield and Sons beginning work on the first floor galleries and roof repairs this month. The redevelopment is part of a multi-million pound project aimed at modernising the venue, which is expected to reopen in summer 2027.

The Greater Manchester construction company will refurbish the first floor gallery spaces, enhancing conditions for artwork and creating brighter, more modern areas for visitors. This phase follows earlier works completed by H H Smith and Sons Ltd, which included the renovation of the ground floor, basement, and initial roof repairs. Those works introduced new flexible performance spaces, a 130-seat studio, meeting rooms, and facilities for education and collections.

Councillor Sue Smith, cabinet member for communities and co-operation at Rochdale Borough Council, expressed enthusiasm for the project, stating, "Appointing Schofield and Sons is another milestone as this project moves into its final year. I am excited to see the improvements to the gallery spaces over the coming months." The redevelopment is funded by the Cultural Development Fund, administered by Arts Council England, alongside contributions from Rochdale Borough Council and The Wolfson Foundation.

The Touchstones project is part of a broader initiative to establish a creative district in Rochdale town centre, which includes the restoration of Rochdale Town Hall and the development of new public art and creative workspaces. The project aims to enhance cultural opportunities for residents and visitors alike, ensuring the venue is fit for the 21st century.

articles.title: Heywood Students Visit First Choice Homes Oldham as Part of Summer School
articles.excerpt: Three teenagers from the Heywood and Middleton North constituency participated in a two-day visit to First Choice Homes Oldham, gaining insights into the housing association's operations and community support.
articles.content_html:
Three teenagers from the Heywood and Middleton North constituency spent two days at First Choice Homes Oldham as part of MP Elsie Blundell's Fly High Summer School.

Emaan Hassan, Caryn-Ann Sarpong-Kumankomah and Charlie Wilcock, all aged 16, were mentored by chief executive Mark Gifford and the senior leadership team. They engaged with various departments, including development, neighbourhood services, sustainability, and the customer contact centre.

First Choice Homes Oldham donated £300 to the Fly High Summer School to support the group's visit to the Houses of Parliament in London. Reflecting on the experience, Emaan Hassan noted the size of the organisation and the number of employees involved, while Caryn-Ann Sarpong-Kumankomah highlighted the importance of understanding how to differentiate oneself in future applications. Charlie Wilcock expressed surprise at the scale of social housing operations and the number of communities served by the organisation.

Mark Gifford stated that the organisation was pleased to host the students and emphasised the importance of such initiatives in helping young people understand democracy. MP Elsie Blundell thanked First Choice Homes Oldham for their support and sponsorship, which facilitated the visit to Parliament. She described the summer school as a means to build confidence and provide opportunities for young people.

articles.content_html:
Councillors in Rochdale have opposed proposed pay rises for themselves, stating their commitment to serving the community rather than seeking financial gain.

The decision comes amid criticism directed at the Labour Party, with claims that the party prioritises financial benefits over the needs of voters.

This stance reflects a broader sentiment among some local representatives who believe that public service should not be financially motivated.

The implications of this decision may influence public perception of the council and its members, particularly in light of ongoing discussions about local governance and accountability.

articles.title: Traffic Updates and Local News from Rochdale
articles.excerpt: Local MPs for Rochdale and Bury North have expressed their support for Prime Minister Andy Burnham's inaugural speech, which outlined plans to address rough sleeping and alleviate cost of living pressures. The Prime Minister also presented a long-term programme for government. Hathershaw College hosted Jim McMahon, MP for Oldham West, Chadderton and Royton…
articles.content_html:
Local MPs for Rochdale and Bury North have expressed their support for Prime Minister Andy Burnham's inaugural speech, which outlined plans to address rough sleeping and alleviate cost of living pressures. The Prime Minister also presented a long-term programme for government.

Hathershaw College hosted Jim McMahon, MP for Oldham West, Chadderton and Royton, to discuss its plans for a new school building and its vision for the future. Meanwhile, Reform UK's candidate for Greater Manchester Mayor has pledged support for a campaign aimed at reopening Bury Castle Armoury.

In community news, Rock Choir has launched a new group in Rochdale, offering local residents the opportunity to join the contemporary singing organisation without the need for auditions or music reading skills.

Additionally, a Bury Council environmental protection officer has been recognised in the ENDS Report Power List 2026 for her contributions to contaminated land management. Three teenagers from the Heywood and Middleton North constituency participated in a two-day programme with First Choice Homes Oldham as part of MP Elsie Blundell's Fly High Summer School.

articles.title: Rochdale Councillors Approve Pay Rise Despite Cost of Living Concerns
articles.excerpt: Rochdale councillors have voted to increase their annual allowances despite objections related to the ongoing cost of living crisis affecting local families.
articles.content_html:
A vote at Rochdale Town Hall has resulted in an increase in the annual allowances for councillors. The current allowance of £12,996.09 will rise to £13,515 following the decision made on July 16.

The pay rise has sparked significant debate among councillors, with members of the Reform and Workers Party voicing strong opposition. Councillor Mohammed Shafiq from the Workers Party described the increase as 'inappropriate' given the financial struggles faced by many families in the borough. He stated that it is not the right time to approve such an increase when so many are struggling to make ends meet.

In response, Labour councillors defended the rise, arguing that the new allowance remains lower than those in other Greater Manchester councils. Councillor Kath Bromfield stated that the increase would help make politics more accessible to those without substantial financial means, while Councillor Dylan Williams noted that the remuneration equates to less than minimum wage considering the time commitment required for councillors.

Council leader Neil Emmott highlighted that those opposed to the increase could choose not to claim the full amount. The vote was passed by the controlling Labour group, with only the basic allowance being adjusted; all other special allowances will remain unchanged. The special allowances include payments for the leader of the council, deputy leaders, cabinet members, and committee chairs, among others.
articles.legal_disclaimer: This article was compiled from identified public sources and may be updated when further verified information becomes available.

articles.title: Rochdale Council Plans Crackdown on Houses of Multiple Occupation
articles.excerpt: Rochdale Council is set to introduce tighter controls on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) following recent protests in Heywood organised by Jamie Thomason
articles.content_html:
ONGOING STORY — Updated 19 July 2026 at 06:28. 3 source updates have been combined into this article.

Rochdale Council is set to introduce tighter controls on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) following recent protests in Heywood organised by Jamie Thomason

Latest update

Rochdale Council is preparing to implement stricter regulations on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) in the borough. This decision follows recent protests against HMOs, particularly in Heywood, where residents have raised issues regarding their impact on local services and community cohesion.

Reform councillors have urged town hall officials to introduce an Article 4 Direction, which would require all HMOs, regardless of size, to obtain specific planning permission. Currently, only HMOs accommodating seven or more people are subject to this requirement. Councillor Paul O’Neill stated that the council aims to alleviate tensions surrounding HMOs in the area.

Councillor Lee Wolf from the Reform group highlighted that while HMO licensing is crucial, it does not address the location of these properties or prevent excessive clustering. He noted that residents want a greater say in the matter, as many believe HMOs are negatively affecting their neighbourhoods.

The Labour group, which currently controls the council, has indicated that it is already working on changes to HMO regulations. Housing boss Councillor Danny Meredith acknowledged the support from Reform for the Labour plan, while Councillor Richard Jackson emphasised the need to follow proper processes before implementing an Article 4 Direction. This directive is expected to be brought before the full council for a formal vote later this year.

Earlier developments

Rochdale Council is set to crack down on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) in the borough. Reform councillors have called on town hall officials to implement stricter regulations. This decision follows protests against HMOs that took place over the weekend in Heywood.

Councillor Paul O’Neill stated that the council aims to introduce an Article 4 Direction to alleviate tensions surrounding HMOs. The issue was raised by Reform councillors during the latest town hall meeting. Article 4 directives would require all HMOs, regardless of size, to obtain specific planning permission. Currently, only HMOs accommodating seven or more people require such permission.

Councillor Lee Wolf from Reform expressed that while HMO licensing is important, it does not address the location of these properties or prevent excessive clustering. He noted that residents desire a greater say in the matter and believe that HMOs are impacting local services, particularly in specific areas.

The controlling Labour group indicated that it is already planning changes. Housing boss Councillor Danny Meredith thanked Reform for their support of the Labour plan. Fellow Labour councillor Richard Jackson added that they are actively working on the issue but must follow proper processes. He emphasised that implementing Article 4 requires substantial evidence and cannot be enacted immediately. The proposal is currently undergoing local authority processes and is expected to be presented to the full council for a formal vote later this year.

Update timeline

  • 19 July 2026, 06:28: Rochdale Council Plans Crackdown on Houses of Multiple Occupation
  • 18 July 2026, 07:43: Rochdale Council to Tighten Controls on Houses of Multiple Occupation

Murphy brings a wealth of experience in local government and is expected to focus on improving services and engaging with the

Murphy brings a wealth of experience in local government and is expected to focus on improving services and engaging with the community.

The appointment follows a thorough selection process, with Murphy emerging as the preferred candidate due to their track record in similar roles.

Council officials are optimistic that Murphy's leadership will help drive forward key initiatives and address the challenges facing the borough.

Earlier developments

Rochdale Borough Council has confirmed Julie Murphy as its new Chief Executive. The appointment received approval during a meeting of the full council.

Julie Murphy will take on the role following the council's decision, which was made public on 17 July 2026.

The new Chief Executive is expected to lead the council in its ongoing efforts to serve the community and manage local services effectively.

Further details regarding her plans and initiatives for the council are anticipated in the coming weeks.

articles.title: Reform UK pushes for borough-wide HMO controls across Rochdale
articles.excerpt: Reform UK councillors have called for stronger planning controls on Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs) across Rochdale Borough, arguing that residents should have a greater say over developments affecting their communities.
articles.summary: A motion brought before Rochdale Council by Reform UK councillors has reignited debate over the growth of HMOs, with the party calling for borough-wide planning restrictions while welcoming council recognition that the issue requires further consideration.
articles.content_html:
Reform UK councillors have renewed calls for stronger controls on Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs) across Rochdale Borough following a debate at a recent Full Council meeting.

The party brought forward a motion proposing a borough-wide Article 4 Direction, which would remove certain permitted development rights and require planning permission before new HMOs could be established.

According to Reform UK, the proposal was intended to give residents a greater opportunity to comment on developments that may affect their neighbourhoods before approval is granted.

Debate over planning powers

During the council debate, the original motion was amended before being considered by councillors.

Reform UK said it would have preferred immediate borough-wide protections but ultimately supported the amended version after it formally acknowledged concerns surrounding increasing HMO concentrations and committed the council to further work on the potential use of Article 4 powers.

The party argued that rejecting the amended motion entirely would have risked losing momentum on an issue that has generated significant discussion among residents in several parts of the borough.

Concerns over community impact

Supporters of the proposal say growing concentrations of HMOs can place additional pressure on local infrastructure, parking, public services and community cohesion.

Reform UK councillors argue that the current planning framework allows many HMOs to be created without the level of scrutiny that residents would expect for other forms of development.

The party believes local people should have a greater voice when significant changes are proposed within established residential communities.

Borough-wide approach proposed

One of the central arguments made during the debate was that restrictions should apply consistently across the borough rather than being introduced only in selected locations.

Reform UK says protecting one neighbourhood while leaving others unaffected could simply relocate development pressure elsewhere.

The party therefore continues to support a borough-wide Article 4 Direction covering Rochdale, Middleton, Heywood and the Pennines.

Issue remains on the political agenda

The councillors say the debate represents an important first step in securing wider recognition of residents' concerns.

While acknowledging that the outcome did not fully reflect their original proposal, Reform UK believes the discussion has ensured that HMO policy remains a live political issue within Rochdale Borough.

The council is expected to continue gathering evidence and reviewing potential planning options before any future decisions are made regarding Article 4 powers.

This article is based on statements published by Reform UK Rochdale Councillors following a Full Council debate regarding HMOs and planning policy. The views expressed are those of the party and form part of an ongoing political discussion concerning housing and planning within Rochdale Borough.
articles.legal_disclaimer: This article reports statements made by elected councillors and political representatives regarding planning policy. The views described are political positions and do not represent adopted council policy unless formally approved by Rochdale Council.

articles.title: Government Responds to Concerns Over Fireworks Distress in Rochdale
articles.excerpt: The Government has acknowledged concerns regarding the anti-social use of loud fireworks in Rochdale, which distress pets, veterans, and older residents, and pose wildfire risks.
articles.content_html:
The Government has responded to concerns regarding the anti-social use of loud fireworks in Rochdale. Local representatives highlighted the distress caused to pets, veterans, and older people, as well as the potential risk of wildfires on the moors.

In a statement, a local councillor expressed satisfaction that the Government has listened to the community's demands for action against the misuse of fireworks. The councillor noted that the issue has been a significant concern for many residents.

The use of loud fireworks has been linked to increased anxiety in pets and distress among vulnerable groups, prompting calls for stricter regulations. The councillor's comments reflect a growing awareness of the impact of fireworks on local communities.

The Government's commitment to address these concerns may lead to new measures aimed at reducing the negative effects of fireworks, although specific details on any forthcoming regulations have yet to be announced.

articles.title: Rochdale Charity Raises Awareness of Honour-Based Abuse at Women’s Event
articles.excerpt: Rochdale Women's Welfare Association participated in the GM4Women Dialogue, focusing on honour-based abuse and women's equality at a Greater Manchester event.
articles.content_html:
ONGOING STORY — Updated 15 July 2026 at 22:40. 2 source updates have been combined into this article.

Rochdale Women's Welfare Association participated in the GM4Women Dialogue, focusing on honour-based abuse and women's equality at a Greater Manchester event.

Latest update

Rochdale Women's Welfare Association participated in the GM4Women Dialogue, focusing on honour-based abuse and women's equality at a Greater Manchester event.

A Rochdale charity has highlighted the issue of honour-based abuse at a recent women’s event in Greater Manchester. The event aimed to raise awareness and provide support for individuals affected by this form of abuse.

Representatives discussed the signs of honour-based abuse and the resources available for victims. They emphasised the importance of community support in addressing this issue.

Attendees were encouraged to engage in discussions about the impact of honour-based abuse. The charity aims to foster a safe environment for those affected to seek help.

Earlier developments

This initiative is part of a broader effort to combat honour-based abuse and promote understanding within the community.

Rochdale Women's Welfare Association participated in the annual GM4Women Dialogue, Deeds and Determination gathering, which brought together women and allies from across Greater Manchester to discuss equality and support for women and girls.

The event included workshops, panel discussions, and contributions from community leaders and elected representatives. The association's Independent Domestic Violence Adviser and Independent Sexual Violence Adviser, Rabiah, joined a plenary panel discussing honour-based violence affecting women and girls. Rabiah also led a workshop with the organisation's Strategic Lead, Rizwana, focusing on honour-based abuse, encouraging discussions on raising awareness and improving support for those affected.

Rochdale's Mayor, Councillor Faisal Rana, and the Mayoress, Mrs Iram Faisal, attended the event, where the Mayor emphasised the importance of supporting women and girls in local communities. Kate Green, Deputy Mayor of Greater Manchester for Safer and Stronger Communities, also attended, alongside Councillors Sameena and Amber Nisa.

Women from all ten Greater Manchester boroughs participated in the gathering, highlighting collaboration between organisations and communities addressing issues affecting women and girls. Rochdale Women's Welfare Association thanked GM4Women for organising the event, stating it provided an opportunity for women to have their voices heard and engage with decision makers.

Rochdale Women's Welfare Association highlighted honour-based abuse awareness during the annual GM4Women Dialogue, Deeds and Determination gathering held in Greater Manchester. The event brought together women and allies from across the region to discuss equality and support for women and girls.

The gathering featured workshops, panel discussions, and contributions from community leaders and elected representatives. The association's Independent Domestic Violence Adviser and Independent Sexual Violence Adviser, Rabiah, participated in a plenary panel discussing issues surrounding honour-based violence. Rabiah also led a workshop with the organisation's Strategic Lead, Rizwana, focusing on honour-based abuse, encouraging discussions on raising awareness and improving support for affected individuals.

The association noted that women from all ten Greater Manchester boroughs attended the event, showcasing collaboration among organisations and communities addressing issues affecting women and girls. Rochdale Women's Welfare Association expressed gratitude to GM4Women for organising the event, which provided a platform for women to share their experiences and engage with decision-makers.
